ground_truth.title: 10 software essays that changed how I think
ground_truth.author: Dr Milan Milanović
ground_truth.main_content: Over the years, I've read a handful of software essays that fundamentally changed my engineering mindset. Each one delivered a lesson that shifted my perspective, from technology choices, architecture, coding practices, and even my career. Below, I share the most impactful essays I learned from each. These range from pragmatic engineering advice to philosophical insights, but all influenced how I think and work as an engineer and leader. The essays covered include: 1. Choose Boring Technology (2015) by Dan McKinley - argues for using proven, reliable technology and treating innovation as a scarce resource. 2. Parse, Don't Validate (2019) by Alexis King - advocates for leveraging static types to make invalid states unrepresentable. 3. Things You Should Never Do, Part I (2000) by Joel Spolsky - warns against rewriting code from scratch, emphasizing the value of legacy code. 4. The Majestic Monolith (2016) by David Heinemeier Hansson - defends monolithic architecture for most products, arguing that microservices aren't always the answer. 5. The Joel Test (2000) by Joel Spolsky - provides a simple 12-point checklist for assessing software team health. 6. How to Design a Good API and Why It Matters (2007) by Joshua Bloch - offers principles for designing lasting, usable APIs. 7. The Rise of Worse is Better (1989) by Richard P. Gabriel - explores why simpler solutions often win in the marketplace. 8. The Grug-Brained Developer (2022) by Carson Gross - provides wisdom about keeping software simple. 9. Software Quality at Top Speed (1996) by Steve McConnell - discusses balancing quality and development speed. 10. Don't Call Yourself a Programmer (2011) by Patrick McKenzie - offers career advice for software professionals.
with: Dan McKinley's Choose Boring Technology was a revelation that permitted me to ignore the hype cycle.
Alexis King's Parse, Don't Validate changed how I think about handling input data in any language.
Joel Spolsky's essay stopped me in my tracks and probably saved me from disastrous rewrites.
DHH's message: Not every problem needs microservices; in fact, most don't.
Public APIs, like diamonds, are forever. You get one chance to get it right, so put in the effort up front.
